# Code Quality Audit

## Summary
- Quality risk score: 3/10 (small codebase; main risk is compatibility drift around Pi internals)
- Bloat score: 2/10 (production source is modest; one lifecycle function is dense but tested)
- Dead exports: 0 confirmed
- Wrapper functions: 0 harmful wrappers confirmed
- Over-abstracted modules: 0 confirmed
- Duplicated code blocks: 1 intentional compatibility clone
- Oversized files/functions: 1 dense production function; no oversized production file
- Test-value risks: 2 integration-maintenance risks

## LOC Estimates vs Reality

| Module | Estimated production LOC | Actual production LOC | Ratio | Verdict |

## Dead Exports / Public Surface

No confirmed dead exports.

Notes:
- `startAsyncJobWithDeps` and helper exports are test seams for lifecycle coverage, not accidental API surface.
- Type exports in `src/types.ts` are internal contracts across modules.
- `src/index.ts` default export is the Pi extension entrypoint from `package.json.pi.extensions`.

## Wrapper Functions

No harmful wrappers found.

Skipped:
- `startAsyncJob()` delegates to `startAsyncJobWithDeps()` but the split keeps production call sites simple while enabling deterministic lifecycle tests.

## Over-Abstracted Modules

None confirmed. The module split maps to lifecycle phases in `edc-context/modules/pi-async-prefix-compaction.md`: hook registration, job lifecycle, preparation, validation, runtime state, utilities, constants/types.

## Duplication

### Intentional Pi preparation clone — possible smell, medium maintenance risk
- where: `src/preparation.ts:14-129`, `src/utils.ts:117-147`, `src/index.test.ts:7-12`
- problem: local code mirrors Pi private preparation/token-accounting behavior because Pi does not publicly export `prepareCompaction()` / `estimateContextTokens()`.
- why it matters: this has already produced parity drift in earlier review rounds; future Pi changes can desync async compaction semantics.
- standard/context: EDC module doc explicitly calls this a compatibility clone and requires parity sentinel tests.
- recommendation: keep the clone narrow and parity-tested; replace it with public Pi exports immediately if Pi exposes them.

## Oversized Files / Functions

### Dense lifecycle orchestrator — possible smell, low-to-medium maintenance risk
- where: `src/job.ts:128-253`
- problem: `startAsyncJobWithDeps` handles threshold gating, ready reuse/replacement, preparation, snapshot creation, timeout management, async result transition, and error transition in one ~120-line function.
- why it matters: the state machine is the riskiest part of the extension; future changes can accidentally break ordering between aborts, timeout cleanup, status updates, and compaction triggers.
- mitigating evidence: lifecycle tests cover pending/ready/failure/timeout/replacement paths, and the function is an explicit orchestrator rather than hidden leaf logic.
- recommendation: if this grows again, split only mechanical transition helpers (`completeReady`, `completeFailure`, timeout setup) without changing behavior; do not refactor now just for aesthetics.

`src/index.test.ts` is large, but it is fixture-heavy and covers real behavior/parity rather than snapshot/mocking theater. No production bloat action needed.

## Deep Call Chains

Entrypoints are shallow:
- `turn_end` → `startAsyncJob` → `startAsyncJobWithDeps` → `prepareAsyncCompaction` / Pi `compact()`
- `session_before_compact` → `validateReadyJob` → `estimateAfterApply`
- command handlers call one formatting/start function each

No simple operation crosses more than four meaningful production hops.

## Test Mirroring / Test Value

### Private Pi sentinel path — low maintenance risk
- where: `src/index.test.ts:7-12`
- problem: tests deep-import `../node_modules/@earendil-works/pi-coding-agent/dist/core/compaction/compaction.js`.
- why it matters: this is valuable parity coverage, but it will break if Pi changes package layout without changing behavior.
- recommendation: keep the comment and test-only scope; switch to public Pi exports if available.

### Long real fixture parity is opt-in — accepted integration tradeoff
- where: `src/index.test.ts:22`, `src/index.test.ts:556-577`, `test-fixtures/edc-real-long-session.jsonl`
- problem: default `bun test` skips the real long fixture parity unless `PI_RUN_REAL_COMPACTION_PARITY=1` is set.
- why it matters: default suite is fast/deterministic, but release verification must remember the opt-in parity command.
- mitigating evidence: `.plans/009-static-compaction-parity-test.md` and `.plans/010-review-artifact-closure.md` record the opt-in command and latest pass.
- recommendation: keep opt-in gating; run it before Pi dependency upgrades/releases.

## Correctness and Error-Handling Risks

No unhandled correctness issue found in current source. Previously reported marker misattribution and duplicated `firstKeptEntryId` drift are fixed:
- marker check: `src/index.ts:108-115`
- result/snapshot boundary check: `src/validation.ts:43-45`

## Interface / Type / Contract Risks

No broad type-safety issue found. `unknown` values from compaction details are narrowed before use in `src/preparation.ts`, `src/utils.ts`, and `src/job.ts`.

Peer dependencies now advertise the tested Pi package family (`>=0.80.3 <0.81.0`) instead of wildcard compatibility.

## Simplicity Opportunities

No urgent simplification needed. The highest-value simplification remains external: delete the local preparation clone if/when Pi exports the required preparation/accounting APIs.
